finding_title: Status critically far below tolerable constraint
defect_description: Brook's Status (15% of submissions with swarm-aware SORA items) is dangerously below Isaac's Tolerable (>= 3 items per aircraft = 80% adoption target). This 65-point gap reflects widespread non-adoption of JARUS SORA v2.5 swarm guidance. Current operators have not integrated swarm-specific failure modes into their risk assessment packages, leaving coordinated-failure risks unanalyzed. This is a regulatory integrity issue: authorizations based on incomplete swarm risk evidence cannot be defensibly granted.
fix_recommendation: Immediate action: CAA publishes exemplary swarm SORA evidence packages (draw from AMLAS case studies); conduct assessor training on JARUS SORA v2.5 swarm-risk taxonomy; Ray's METER-003 enforces minimum evidence requirements (>= 3 distinct items per aircraft addressing loss-of-separation, emergent failure, coordination mechanism). Medium-term: JARUS SORA v2.5 adoption curve acceleration via EUROCAE WG6 guidance publication; monthly CAA assessor feedback loop: what swarm-aware evidence impressed assessors becomes public guidance.
cross_references: Brook benchmarks: Status 15% vs Ideal 80%; Isaac Constraint: >= 3 items tolerable level; Ray METER-003: Swarm-Specific Risk Evidence Inventory Audit; Raj Background: SwarmAwareSORAEvidence critical priority rank 2

finding_title: Largest gap in analysis: status alarmingly below tolerable
defect_description: Brook's Status (28% of safety-critical ML decisions reconstructable) is dangerously close to Isaac's Survival threshold (70%) and far below Tolerable (80%). The 62-point gap from Status to Ideal (90%) is the single-largest quality risk in this requirement set. This reflects widespread lack of ML explainability instrumentation: operators rely on high-performance neural networks (opaque) without LIME/SHAP/saliency-map explanations. Post-incident investigation will be blocked if AAIB cannot reconstruct ML decisions. This is an investigability crisis.
fix_recommendation: Immediate action: AMLAS guidance mandates explainability by model type (decision trees 100%, SVMs feature importance + boundary, neural networks LIME/SHAP >= 80% coverage). Ray's METER-011 tests reconstruction by independent reviewer (>= 80% target). Recommend decision-tree or SVM models for safety-critical decisions; reserve neural networks for non-critical optimization. Freeze model development post-authorization; all ML components require re-certification if updated. Short-term: industry adoption of LIME/SHAP tooling and AAIB acceptance of proxy explanations.
cross_references: Brook benchmarks: Status 28% vs Tolerable 80% vs Ideal 90%; Isaac Survival: 70% (system near unassurable threshold); Ray METER-011: ML Decision Reconstruction Forensic Test; Raj Background: MLForensicExplainability critical priority rank 8; Tom Target: Wish/Goal/Stretch targets unreachable without Status improvement

finding_title: Status below tolerable; workload bounds significantly deficient
defect_description: Brook's Status (composite score 52/100) is 18 points below Isaac's Tolerable (70). Largest deficit is WorkloadBounds: Status 35/100 vs Tolerable 100. Swarm operations generate multiple concurrent events (engine failure, C2 loss, emergent-behavior anomaly) that exceed human crew capacity to monitor and respond. Scale requires SituationalAwareness >= 2Hz nominal / >= 10Hz off-nominal; AuthorityPreservation >= 80% reversible / 100% irreversible; TakeoverLatency >= 10s; WorkloadBounds <= 3 concurrent alarms, <= 6 min active control/hour. Current HMI designs do not meet these thresholds for multi-aircraft operations.
fix_recommendation: HMI redesign critical: (1) Auto-triage alerts—display only 1-2 most critical concurrently; (2) Progressive disclosure—alert details on demand, not auto-display; (3) Swarm-level abstraction—show formation state, not per-aircraft state; (4) Pre-positioned crew takeover—system proposes action, crew approves in 0.5s cycle. Ray's METER-013 validates workload in simulation before authorization. NASA-TLX workload measurement required. Mandatory crew training on multi-event prioritization.
cross_references: Brook benchmarks: Status 52 vs Tolerable 70; Isaac Constraint: composite 70 required; Ray METER-012, METER-013: Crew State-Update Frequency, Crew Workload & Takeover Latency Simulation Test; Raj Background: MeaningfulHumanControl critical priority rank 9, major workload risk

finding_title: Status significantly below tolerable; inter-rater clarity insufficient
defect_description: Brook's Status (55% clarity) is 30 points below Isaac's Tolerable (85%). Two independent safety-case reviewers do not agree consistently on what constitutes 'clear' ConOps. Current ConOps documents define nominal behavior but omit explicit worst-credible boundaries for each operational phase. This ambiguity cascades: crew training is ad-hoc, geofence/TDA margins are conservative (to close gaps), and safety case hazard analysis is incomplete. Scale requires inter-rater reliability (Cohen's kappa >= 0.8) and explicit worst-credible definitions per phase.
fix_recommendation: Publish ConOps template with mandatory sections: (1) Nominal behavior quantified (e.g., formation tolerance +/- 10m, heading variance <= 5°), (2) Worst-credible boundary explicit (mean + 3-sigma or absolute physics limit), (3) Recovery procedure for each off-nominal phase. Ray's METER-004 (Inter-Rater Clarity Consensus Test) becomes standard gate: dual independent review with structured questionnaire, kappa >= 0.8 requirement. Exemplar swarm ConOps library published (AMLAS case studies). ASTM F3642 clarity guidance adoption mandatory.
cross_references: Brook benchmarks: Status 55% vs Tolerable 85%; Isaac Constraint: 85% required; Ray METER-004: Inter-Rater Clarity Consensus Test; Alan Stakeholders: Safety-Case Reviewer, Flight-Ops Inspector, Airspace Regulator dependent on ConOps clarity; Tom Targets: Wish 80%, Goal 90%, Stretch 95% (all unreachable without status improvement)

finding_title: Status significantly below critical regulatory minimum
defect_description: Brook's Status (88% encounter resolution success) is 11.5 points below Isaac's Tolerable (99.5%) and 11.9 points below Isaac's Regulatory minimum (99.9% per DO-365 MOPS). This gap reflects inadequate non-cooperative encounter detection: civil radars struggle with small-RCS swarms. Scale requires >= 99.5% encounter resolution success (>= 500ft horizontal recovery, zero secondary conflicts, >= 95% decision consistency across repeat encounters). Current systems do not meet this standard, meaning BVLOS trials in Class G airspace with civil traffic cannot be authorized.
fix_recommendation: Algorithm development required: (1) Invest in non-cooperative radar detection algorithm maturity—current physics limits are 70-90% detection; target 99%+ via sensor fusion (radar + ADS-B + visual). (2) Ray's METER-008 (DO-365 Encounter Resolution Test Matrix) runs >= 200 scenarios; analyze failures and iterate. (3) Extend testing to swarm-formation-aware encounters: does DAA logic respond consistently if formation is tight vs loose? (4) Define swarm DAA protocol: lead triggers resolution, followers execute coordinated maneuver. High development investment required; current status makes BVLOS authorization impossible.
cross_references: Brook benchmarks: Status 88% vs Tolerable 99.5% vs Regulatory 99.9%; Isaac Constraint: 99.5% tolerable, 99.9% regulatory; Ray METER-008: DO-365 Encounter Resolution Test Matrix; Raj Background: DAAPerformance critical priority rank 6, 11% gap to tolerable

finding_title: Status alarmingly low; REQ017 missing user story indicates regulatory deprioritization
defect_description: Brook's Status (2 stakeholder groups engaged) is 3 groups below Isaac's Tolerable (5 groups minimum per CAP 1616). REQ017 has NO USER STORY in the requirements specification, indicating explicit regulatory deprioritization of public engagement. However, CAP 1616 airspace change process and GDPR data-protection law implicitly mandate community consultation. Swarm operations will generate significant public concern (noise, privacy, safety); current engagement practices are inadequate. Gap reflects social-license risk: community opposition triggered by poor engagement can block trial approval.
fix_recommendation: Elevate public engagement to mandatory authorization criterion (not optional). Publish public-engagement template: (1) Identify 5+ stakeholder groups (residents within 5km, landowners, environmental, aviation, media); (2) Issue prior notification >= 30 days before first flight; (3) Conduct structured feedback (survey, workshop, open forum); (4) Document response to all feedback (acceptance, mitigation, or rejection with rationale). Ray's METER-019 (CAP 1616 Consultation Audit) becomes standard gate. Proactive media strategy required: early notification, safety messaging, site visits during trial, incident-response protocol publication. Establish baseline social-license metrics (community satisfaction, misinformation detection).
cross_references: Brook benchmarks: Status 2 groups vs Tolerable 5 vs Ideal 6; Isaac Constraint: 5 groups tolerable, 6 regulatory (populated areas); Ray METER-019: CAP 1616 Consultation Audit & Community Engagement Report; Raj Background: PublicEngagementCoverage priority rank 15, significant social-license risk; DeFOSPAM Observation-12: REQ017 absent user story

finding_title: Novel domain with nascent practice; status far below required test coverage
defect_description: Brook's Status (3 emergent behaviors identified) is far below Tolerable (95% of test flight hours within bounds). This is not a simple gap—it reflects the fundamental immaturity of swarm assurance. The scale conflates two metrics: behavior identification (3 behaviors present) vs test coverage (95% of hours in bounds). Current status measures only inventory; lacks test evidence. No standard taxonomy of emergent swarm behaviors exists (EUROCAE WG6 draft 2026). Without explicit inventory of expected behaviors and quantified boundaries, trial cannot assess whether observed swarm dynamics are safe or anomalous.
fix_recommendation: Urgent: EUROCAE WG6 swarm-behavior taxonomy development (target 2026 publication). Interim: AMLAS case studies establish accepted behaviors. Ray's METER-009 (Emergent Behavior Boundary Documentation Audit) requires operator to identify >= 5 distinct behaviors (formation, coordination latency, flocking, decision deadlock, split/rejoin) with quantified boundaries. Ray's METER-010 (Emergent Behavior Flight Test Coverage Validator) accumulates evidence: >= 95% of flight hours in bounds, out-of-bounds triggers investigation. Define decision policy: if behavior exceeds bounds in > 5% of flights, trial pauses pending investigation. Establish baseline compliance monitoring.
cross_references: Brook benchmarks: Status 3 behaviors vs Tolerable 5 vs Ideal 8; Isaac Constraint: 95% test coverage within bounds; Ray METER-009, METER-010: Emergent Behavior audits; Raj Background: SwarmEmergentBoundedness critical priority rank 7, largest novel-domain gap; Tom Targets: Wish 5, Goal 6, Stretch 7 (all achievable if Tolerable is clarified)

finding_title: 20% ambiguous responsibility assignments for novel domains
defect_description: Brook's Status (80% coverage) vs Tolerable (100% gap-free) indicates 20% ambiguous responsibility assignments, particularly for swarm-specific domains. The scale requires binary outcome: either 100% gap-free (exactly one owner per aspect, no overlaps) or not. Current matrices lack clear apportionment for: swarm-aware SORA assessment (CAA vs MAA?), emergent-behavior assurance (who signs off?), ML explainability (CAA + AAIB but not MAA?). This creates regulatory risk: operators don't know who to answer to; delayed authorization cycles result.
fix_recommendation: CAA/MAA joint task force publishes apportionment guidance for swarm-specific domains within 90 days: (1) Swarm-aware SORA assessment = CAA (primary); (2) Emergent-behavior assurance = CAA + joint autonomy reviewer; (3) ML explainability = CAA + AAIB (investigative interest), not MAA unless classified. Ray's METER-017 (Regulatory Responsibility Traceability Matrix Audit) becomes mandatory pre-case-opening gate: verifies 100% coverage, zero overlaps, both CAA/MAA signatures present. For classified trials (MOD involvement), establish classified-apportionment process (sanitized summary for CAA, classified annex retained by MAA, joint panel review in secure facility).
cross_references: Brook benchmarks: Status 80% vs Tolerable 100% (binary); Isaac Constraint: 100% required; Ray METER-017: Regulatory Responsibility Traceability Matrix Audit; Raj Background: CAAMAAApportionmentGapFreeness priority rank 13, governance issue not technical

finding_title: Nascent governance model; status far below achievable targets
defect_description: Brook's Status (2.5 well-defined gates) is far below Tolerable (>= 4 gates) and Ideal (>= 6 gates with reversibility). This is a governance/process defect, not a technical one. Most trials use forward-only gates (pass/fail) rather than reversible gates (exit criteria, re-entry procedures, data preservation). Scale requires all gates to be reversible by design: if Phase 3 discovers emergent-behavior anomaly, trial pauses (not cancels), emerges investigation, re-entry criteria defined. Current practice risks: unexpected findings trigger program cancellation instead of learning cycles.
fix_recommendation: Publish phased-trial template: Phase 1 (ground), Phase 2 (VLOS single), Phase 3 (VLOS swarm), Phase 4 (BVLOS nominal), Phase 5 (BVLOS swarm). Ray's METER-018 validates reversibility: each gate has (1) clear criterion (e.g., 10 successful flights, zero emergent-behavior exceedances), (2) exit criterion (e.g., 1 out-of-bounds event detected), (3) re-entry criteria (e.g., 5 additional flights with new design before re-try). Mandate write-once evidence storage or comprehensive audit-logging: all phase data preserved indefinitely. Policy clarity: if behavior exceeds bounds in > 5% of flights, trial pauses (reversible), not cancels (terminal).
cross_references: Brook benchmarks: Status 2.5 gates vs Tolerable >=4 vs Ideal >=6; Ray METER-018: Phased Gate Criteria & Reversibility Procedure Specification; Raj Background: PhasedGateReversibility priority rank 14, programme management enabler

finding_title: Metric conflation: survival time vs link availability; definition ambiguity
defect_description: Alexa's Scale specifies time-based metric (>= 30 seconds autonomous survival). Isaac's Constraint specifies availability metric (>= 99% link availability). These measure different things: survival time = duration after link loss before unsafe state; availability = percentage of mission time link is operational. Scale and Constraint are not directly comparable. Raj's Background notes this conflation as a risk. Brook's Status (45-second survival) meets Survival threshold (30s) but doesn't clarify link availability percentage. Ambiguity creates validation gap: which metric dominates authorization decision?
fix_recommendation: Clarify metrics: (1) Survival time = autonomous safe-state duration after complete C2 loss (>= 30s minimum per scale); (2) Link availability = percentage of mission time C2 link is operational (>= 99% per Isaac). Ray's METER-007 measures both independently: autonomous survival time AND link availability percentage. Update scale or constraint to eliminate ambiguity. Recommend: primary metric is 99% availability (crew reliance); secondary metric is 30s autonomous survival (failover assurance). Both must pass for authorization.
cross_references: Alexa Scale: >= 30 seconds autonomous survival (time-based); Isaac Constraint: >= 99% link availability (percentage-based); Ray METER-007: Link Loss Survival Hold-Safe Validation (measures both); Brook benchmarks: Status 45 seconds (only survival, not availability); Raj Background: C2LinkResilience medium risk on metric conflation

finding_title: Swarm-operations endorsement nascent; target 1.5 competencies below tolerable
defect_description: Brook's Status (3.5 documented competencies per crew member) is 1.5 competencies below Isaac's Tolerable (5). Current crew licensing requires: CAA UAS operator cert, aircraft type rating, operational endorsement. Swarm-operations endorsement is nascent and not yet widely deployed (emerging in 2026). Scale requires 5: certification (UAS operator + swarm endorsement + type rating), role-specific training (pass/fail documented), emergency-procedure simulation (>= 90% success), minimum flight hours (>= 50 pilot, >= 20 sensor op). Gap reflects training curriculum lag.
fix_recommendation: CAA/JARUS publish swarm-operations endorsement training curriculum (timeline 2026). Pilot training providers offer endorsement courses. Interim: grandfather early pilots under alternative pathways (AMLAS trial participation = swarm-ops endorsement equivalent). Standard emergency procedures required: link-loss recovery, geofence-breach response, DAA encounter handling, swarm-member incapacitation. Ray's METER-016 validates >= 90% simulation proficiency before authorization. Allow simulator hours toward experience requirement (10 hours simulator = 10 hours operational). Gap is achievable through training standardization.
cross_references: Brook benchmarks: Status 3.5 competencies vs Tolerable 5 vs Record 6; Isaac Constraint: 5 required; Ray METER-016: Crew Emergency Procedure Simulation Validation; Raj Background: CrewCompetencyEvidence priority rank 12, training-dependent improvement

finding_title: Status at baseline; 28-point gap to tolerable indicates persistent rework cycles
defect_description: Brook's Status (72% first-submission pack completeness) is 28 points below Isaac's Tolerable (100%). This means ~28% of submissions are rejected on first Readiness Check and must be reworked. DeFOSPAM Dorothy-8 identified ambiguity: 'complete' has two meanings (REQ001: application intake completeness vs REQ012: forensic data logging completeness). Alexa distinguished these with different scale parameters. However, 28% rework rate indicates operators lack clear definition of what constitutes a 'complete' pack before submission. This cascades: downstream reviews (SORA, safety case, crew training) cannot commence until pack passes gate.
fix_recommendation: Publish prescriptive document checklist (per operation type: PDRA-01 VLOS, BVLOS Swarm SAIL-V, etc.) in CAA guidance before intake opens. Implement Ray's METER-001 (Artefact Detector Quick Gate): automated file presence/absence detection, flagging gaps within 10 minutes of submission. Mandatory documents: ConOps (>= 5 pages, explicit worst-credible boundaries), SORA (>= 3 pages, swarm-aware risk evidence), Safety Case (>= 10 pages, hazard coverage), Training Plan, Personnel List, Environmental Assessment. Offer pre-submission consultation to reduce rejection rate. Target: 90% first-submission success within 9 months (Tom Wish), 100% within 12 months (Tom Goal).
cross_references: Brook benchmarks: Status 72% vs Tolerable 100%; Isaac Constraint: binary gate (100% pass or rework); Ray METER-001: Artefact Detector Quick Gate; Ray METER-002: Paragraph-Level Coverage Audit (Thorough Gate); Raj Background: PackCompleteness critical priority rank 1, foundational gate; Tom Targets: Wish 90%, Goal 100%

finding_title: Status exceeds tolerable; minimal gap but swarm multiplication risk unaddressed
defect_description: Brook's Status (99.6% containment) is ABOVE Isaac's Tolerable (99.5%), suggesting technology maturity. However, the Scale measures single-aircraft containment via Monte Carlo. Swarm operations multiply risk: probability that AT LEAST ONE aircraft breaches geofence increases combinatorially (5 aircraft: ~5 x single-breach probability). Scale does not explicitly address multi-aircraft correlation. Raj's Background notes this: 'Swarm ops increase breach probability combinatorially.' Status (99.6%) may mask aggregated risk when formalized as swarm.
fix_recommendation: Extend scale to explicitly measure multi-aircraft containment: test all N aircraft simultaneously during Monte Carlo; measure probability that none breach. For 5-aircraft swarm: each aircraft at 99.6% means swarm at ~97.8% containment (5 independent failures). Tolerable constraint may need elevation (99.95% single-aircraft to achieve 99% swarm). Ray's METER-006 (Live Fly-Away Breach Test) must include full-swarm dynamics: do formation interactions (e.g., wake effects, RF interference) degrade individual geofence performance? Continuous post-flight monitoring (METER-010 pattern) tracks real-world performance; if trend moves below Tolerable, trial pauses.
cross_references: Alexa Scale: Monte Carlo 99.9% success across single/dual failures; Brook benchmarks: Status 99.6% vs Tolerable 99.5% vs Survival 99.0% vs Regulatory 99.9%; Isaac Constraint: multiple levels per populated area; Ray METER-005, METER-006: Monte Carlo + Live Fly-Away Test; Raj Background: GeofenceContainment notes swarm multiplication risk

finding_title: Status slightly below tolerable; multi-aircraft coordination gap
defect_description: Brook's Status (99.1% effectiveness) is 0.8 points below Isaac's Tolerable (99.9%). This is a modest gap suggesting technology maturity (98% baseline in FY 2024 improved to 99.1%). However, Scale measures single-aircraft termination via FMEA-derived fault injection. Swarms add complexity: if one aircraft terminates while others continue, mid-air collision risk during synchronized descent. Scale does not specify multi-aircraft termination protocol or test coverage. Raj's Background notes this: 'Swarm termination is coordinated: if one aircraft terminates while others continue, mid-air collision risk during descent.'
fix_recommendation: Define swarm termination protocol: (1) Trigger fires on one aircraft (independent termination system); (2) Broadcast termination command to swarm via inter-vehicle link; (3) All aircraft execute synchronized descent with separation maintained (not free-fall). Ray's METER-014 (FMEA-Derived Fault-Injection Test) must include multi-aircraft scenarios: terminate one aircraft while others continue; verify no mid-air collisions. Expand fault modes to include inter-vehicle link loss during termination (formation breakup risk). Test >= 50 failure scenarios including multi-aircraft termination sequences. Continuous post-flight monitoring tracks termination reliability; if trend moves below 99%, trial pauses.
cross_references: Alexa Scale: >= 99.9% single-aircraft effectiveness; Brook benchmarks: Status 99.1% vs Tolerable 99.9% vs Survival 99.0%; Isaac Constraint: 99.9% required for first flight; Ray METER-014: Independent Termination Fault-Injection Test; Raj Background: IndependentFlightTermination notes multi-aircraft coordination gap

finding_title: Status below tolerable; swarm data-volume management unaddressed
defect_description: Brook's Status (88% incident data completeness) is 12 points below Isaac's Tolerable (100%). Scale requires complete recorded evidence (all state variables >= 10Hz, data integrity no gaps > 100ms, forensic provenance verified, retention through MOR investigation). Status gap (12%) indicates ~12% of incidents have logging gaps or missing provenance certification. For swarms, data volume multiplies: 5-aircraft swarm logging ~100+ state variables at >= 10Hz generates gigabytes per hour. Storage, transmission, and forensic provenance management become challenging. Scale does not explicitly address swarm-specific logging schema (per-aircraft state, inter-vehicle link state, formation geometry).
fix_recommendation: Define swarm logging schema: (1) Full-resolution for safety-critical variables (position XYZ, velocity, autonomy mode, engine status, crew inputs); (2) Aggregated/compressed for non-critical (sensor calibration, motor RPM, diagnostics). Ray's METER-015 (Data Recording Completeness & Integrity Audit) expands to swarm: pre-flight checks all logging systems (per-aircraft + formation-level metrics), post-flight validates data integrity. Redundant logging systems reduce gap probability. Standardize forensic provenance (SHA-256 hashing, atomic-clock timestamps, digitally signed metadata). EUROCAE WG6 may develop guidance (check 2026 timeline). Compression post-flight for archive; real-time transmission via high-bandwidth link (not feasible for swarm-scale data).
cross_references: Alexa Scale: Complete recorded evidence all variables >= 10Hz, no gaps > 100ms; Brook benchmarks: Status 88% vs Tolerable 100%; Isaac Constraint: 100% required (binary: either fully logged or investigation compromised); Ray METER-015: Data Recording Completeness & Integrity Audit
